label: Yehezkel (Ezekiel) — Part 3 — Chapters 20–24
summary_plain: The elders of Yisra'EL come to inquire of YHWH. YHWH responds by recounting the full history of Yisra'EL's rebellion — from Mitsrayim through the wilderness to the land. In Mitsrayim they would not cast away their idols. In the wilderness they rejected YHWH's statutes. In the land they sacrificed on every high hill. YHWH declares: I will be king over you with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm and with wrath poured out. The sword of YHWH is sharpened and polished — it is given for slaughter. YHWH sets the cooking pot on the fire — Yerushalayim is the pot, the people are the meat, and the rust cannot be cleaned out. On the day Yerushalayim falls, YHWH takes Yehezkel's wife — the delight of his eyes — and commands him not to mourn, as a sign to the people.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from the DSS Ezekiel fragments (4QEzek-a, 4QEzek-b, 4QEzek-c) and the Orit Ge'ez. Chapter 20 is the most comprehensive recounting of Yisra'EL's rebellion history in the prophetic literature — more detailed than the historical psalms (78, 105, 106). YHWH reviews three stages of rebellion: in Mitsrayim, in the wilderness, in the land — each time the people reject YHWH's statutes. Chapter 22 catalogs the specific charges: bloodshed, idol worship, sexual violations, oppression of the stranger, desecration of the Shabbat. Chapter 24 introduces the cooking pot parable — Yerushalayim is corroded metal that cannot be cleansed. The death of Yehezkel's wife (24:15-27) is a prophetic sign-act: the prophet's personal grief becomes the vehicle for YHWH's message.

key_terms.definition: YHWH recounts Yisra'EL's rebellion in three stages: in Mitsrayim they kept their idols, in the wilderness they rejected the statutes, in the land they sacrificed on high places. Each stage follows the same pattern: YHWH acts, the people rebel, YHWH relents for the sake of his name.

Chapter 24: Yerushalayim is a cooking pot set on fire. The meat is boiled. The rust (filth) in the pot cannot be cleaned out — it has been heated empty until the copper glows. The corrosion is permanent. The pot itself must be destroyed.

YHWH tells Yehezkel: I am about to take from you the delight of your eyes (24:16). His wife dies, and YHWH commands him not to mourn — no weeping, no tears, no mourning bread. The prophet's personal loss becomes YHWH's sign to the people about the coming loss of the Temple.

fill_blank.prompt: As I live, declares YHWH, surely with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm and with wrath poured out I will be ______ over you.
fill_blank.source_quote: As I live, declares YHWH, surely with a mighty hand and an outstretched arm and with wrath poured out I will be king over you.

fill_blank.prompt: Son of man, behold, I am about to take from you the delight of your ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: Son of man, behold, I am about to take from you the delight of your eyes with a stroke — yet you shall not mourn nor weep, nor shall your tears run down.

fill_blank.prompt: I sought for a man among them who should build up the wall and stand in the ______ before me for the land, that I should not destroy it — but I found none.
fill_blank.source_quote: And I sought for a man among them who should build up the wall and stand in the breach before me for the land, that I should not destroy it — but I found none.

multiple_choice.question: According to YHWH's recounting in chapter 20, when did Yisra'EL's rebellion begin?
options: In Mitsrayim — they would not cast away their idols
multiple_choice.source_quote: On the day when I chose Yisra'EL, I swore to the offspring of the house of Ya'aqov — I made myself known to them in the land of Mitsrayim. But they rebelled against me and would not listen — they did not cast away the detestable things their eyes feasted on.

multiple_choice.question: What did YHWH seek and fail to find?
options: A prophet to send to Babylon
A man to stand in the breach for the land
A righteous king to replace Tsidqiyahu
A priest to cleanse the Temple
multiple_choice.source_quote: I sought for a man among them who should build up the wall and stand in the breach before me for the land, that I should not destroy it — but I found none.

multiple_choice.question: Why was Yehezkel commanded not to mourn when his wife died?
options: Because mourning was forbidden in Babylon
As a prophetic sign — the people would lose the Temple and be unable to mourn normally
multiple_choice.source_quote: Speak to the house of Yisra'EL: Behold, I will profane my sanctuary, the pride of your power, the delight of your eyes — and your sons and daughters whom you left behind shall fall by the sword.

faq.question: What does 'I sought for a man to stand in the breach but found none' (22:30) mean for the Great Eraser's impact?
faq.answer: YHWH searched for someone to stand in the breach — to repair the wall, to intercede, to confront the corruption — and found no one. The charges in chapter 22 are specific: the princes shed blood, the priests violate Torah, the prophets whitewash, the people oppress the stranger and wrong the poor. Every level of leadership failed. The Great Eraser operates the same way in every generation: it removes the covenant people's ability to produce leaders who stand in the breach. The Slave Bible removed the prophetic texts that teach confrontation of power. European theological seminaries trained pastors in institutional compliance, not prophetic resistance. The educational systems that should produce breach-standers instead produce gatekeepers who protect the wall's cracks with whitewash. When YHWH searches for someone to stand in the breach and finds none, the judgment falls. The restoration of the breach-stander tradition — through direct access to the covenant texts in their oldest recoverable form — is the purpose of the ACR.

faq.question: Why does YHWH recount the full rebellion history when the elders come to inquire?
faq.answer: The elders come to Yehezkel seeking a word from YHWH (20:1). YHWH refuses to be inquired of by them — instead he recounts the entire history of rebellion from Mitsrayim through the wilderness to the land. The pattern is: YHWH chose them, they rebelled, YHWH relented for the sake of his name, they rebelled again. Three complete cycles. The point is that the current destruction is not arbitrary — it is the culmination of a documented pattern. But YHWH concludes with restoration: 'I will bring you into the wilderness of the peoples and enter into judgment with you face to face' (20:35). The history lesson serves the future: the covenant people must know their own pattern of rebellion to break it. The institutions that control covenant education — from the Rabbinate to the seminaries — have no interest in teaching this pattern because they are often the false prophets plastering walls with whitewash.
